perturbation.original_text: Company agrees that it will reta in all Information provided in connection with the RFP in the strictest confidence, and will neither use nor di sclose such Information to anyone other than those who, in each case, need to know the Information for the purpose of responding to this RFP. Company will use its best efforts to ensure th at such individuals adhere to the NDA, and Company agrees that it will be responsible for any breach of this NDA by any of its employees, agents, authorized representatives or permitted assignees or subcontractors.
perturbation.changed_text: Company agrees that it will retain Information provided in connection with the RFP in a manner it deems reasonably confidential, and will endeavor not to use or disclose such Information to anyone other than those with a legitimate interest in the RFP response. Company will attempt to ensure that such individuals are generally aware of the NDA, and Company agrees that it will address any breach of this NDA by its employees, agents, authorized representatives or permitted assignees or subcontractors in a manner it considers appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates 'strictest confidence' and 'best efforts', creating a clear obligation. The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing 'strictest confidence' with 'a manner it deems reasonably confidential,' 'best efforts' with 'attempt to ensure', and 'will be responsible' with 'will address in a manner it considers appropriate.' This weakens the obligation and introduces subjective judgment.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text's weakened confidentiality obligations contradict California Civil Code § 1798.81.5, which mandates that businesses implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices appropriate to the nature of the information to protect personal information. The ambiguous wording 'a manner it deems reasonably confidential' and 'will attempt to ensure' falls short of the legally required 'reasonable security procedures and practices'.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.81. CUSTOMER RECORDS [1798.80 - 1798.84] ( Title 1.81 added by Stats. 2000, Ch. 1039, Sec. 1. ) 1798.81.5. (a) (1) It is the intent of the Legislature to ensure that personal information about California residents is protected. To that end, the purpose of this section is to encourage businesses that own, license, or maintain personal information about Californians to provide reasonable security for that information. (2) For the purpose of this section, the terms “own” and “license” include personal information that a business retains as part of the business’ internal customer account or for the purpose of using that information in transactions with the person to whom the information relates. The term “maintain” includes personal information that a business maintains but does not own or license. (b) A business that owns, licenses, or maintains personal information about a California resident shall implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices appropriate to the nature of the information, to protect the personal information fro
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity in the confidentiality obligations, weakening the standard of care required. The original text specifies 'strictest confidence' and 'best efforts,' while the changed text uses phrases like 'a manner it deems reasonably confidential' and 'attempt to ensure.' This directly contradicts the intent of California Civil Code § 1798.81.5, as described in snippet 1, which states businesses 'shall implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices appropriate to the nature of the information, to protect the personal information.' The 'reasonable security procedures and practices' required by law implies a higher standard than 'a manner it deems reasonably confidential' and 'attempt to ensure' suggests, leading to a legal contradiction because the contract now potentially falls short of the legally required security measures.

perturbation.original_text: Upon submission of a proposal to the RFP, or if the State Bar so requests, whichever occurs first, Company will promptly, and at its own expense: (i ) redeliver to the State Bar all copies of the Information in Company’s possession, whether such Information is in written, electronic, digital or other form or format; (ii) destroy any a nd all analyses, compilations, studies, or other documents in any form or format that were prep ared by or for the use of Company which contain or reflect any Information; and (iii) certify such destruction to the State Bar in writing by an authorized officer of the Company who supervised such destruction, as well as the return of all copies as specified above.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon submission of a proposal to the RFP, or if the State Bar so requests, whichever occurs first, Company will endeavor to redeliver to the State Bar readily accessible copies of the Information in Company’s possession, and may destroy analyses, compilations, studies, or other documents that were prepared for the use of Company which contain or reflect any Information, and consider providing certification of such actions to the State Bar.
perturbation.explanation: The original text sets a firm obligation for the company to return and destroy the information and certify this act. The modification introduces ambiguity by changing 'will promptly, and at its own expense' to 'will endeavor', changing 'redeliver to the State Bar all copies' to 'redeliver to the State Bar readily accessible copies', replacing 'destroy any and all analyses, compilations, studies, or other documents' with 'may destroy analyses, compilations, studies, or other documents', and replacing 'certify such destruction to the State Bar' with 'consider providing certification'. This weakens the obligation and makes compliance discretionary.
perturbation.law_explanation: While this code speaks to the general scope of discovery, the altered NDA language weakens the obligation of a company to destroy and return the information in a way that impedes the State Bar's ability to enforce its rights related to discovery. By introducing 'endeavor', 'readily accessible' and 'may', the obligation becomes nebulous and unenforceable, which reduces the protection the State Bar would have under civil procedure rules in a lawsuit related to misuse of information.

MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 4. CIVIL DISCOVERY ACT [2016.010 - 2036.050] ( Title 4 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) CHAPTER 2. Scope of Discovery [2017.010 - 2017.320] ( Chapter 2 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) ARTICLE 1. General Provisions [2017.010 - 2017.020] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) 2017.020. (a) The court shall limit the scope of discovery if it determines that the burden, expense, or intrusiveness of that discovery clearly outweighs the likelihood that the information sought will lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. The court may make this determination pursuant to a motion for protective order by a party or other affected person. This motion shall be accompanied by a meet and confer declaration under Section 2016.040. (b) The court shall impose a monetary sanction under Chapter 7 (commencing with Section 2023.010) against any party, person, or attorney who unsuccessfully makes or opposes a motion for a protective order, unless it finds that the one subject to the sanction acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust. (c) (1) Notwithstanding sub
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity that weakens the original firm obligation, creating a legal contradiction. The original text used definitive language like 'will promptly' and 'shall,' while the modified text employs discretionary terms like 'endeavor,' 'readily accessible,' and 'may.' The Perturbation Explanation makes clear that this shift introduces ambiguity and makes compliance optional. The Law Explanation indicates that this change impedes the State Bar's ability to enforce its rights related to discovery. Scraped Law Snippet 1 relates to discovery, and while it doesn't explicitly state the requirements for returning or destroying information, the modified text's ambiguity weakens the enforceability of any agreement concerning the scope of discovery. The original text created a clear obligation, the changed text undermines it.
